skill.name: tool-design
skill.description: Guides teams in designing clear, reliable tool interfaces for AI agents.
skill.summary: Design agent tools with clear contracts, recoverable errors, and reduced ambiguity.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command, network, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts were mostly false positives from markdown prose and example code. The real concern is design guidance that recommends broad filesystem access and command-execution style tooling, which can be risky without sandboxing and approvals.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem and Command Tooling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends direct filesystem access through a single command execution tool and standard Unix utilities. This is design guidance rather than executable code, but implementations need least privilege, sandboxing, and approvals.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly recommends command execution and filesystem access as an agent design pattern. The risk is architectural guidance, not a confirmed exploit or runnable payload.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command detections occur inside inline markdown or fenced Python examples. No executable shell command, Ruby backtick execution, or installed script was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are markdown examples and text formatting. They are not executable files and do not invoke a shell.

low_findings.title: Network Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The network detections are a source URL and prose about agent interfaces. No HTTP client call, data upload, credential handling, or external request logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only URL is metadata, and the other lines are explanatory prose. I found no code that imports HTTP libraries or performs network I/O.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Term-Collision False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated weak-cryptography alerts match ordinary documentation text and metadata, not cryptographic functions. No MD5, SHA1, cipher use, or password hashing code was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains documentation and illustrative snippets only. I found no evidence of cryptographic API usage in the reviewed skill file.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Conceptual Examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections come from advice mentioning standard Unix utilities and naming examples. They do not run commands or collect host information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms appear in explanatory design guidance and anti-pattern examples. There is no executable reconnaissance workflow in the skill.

content.value_statement: Agent tools often fail because their contracts are unclear or too fragmented. This skill gives practical patterns for tool descriptions, schemas, errors, and tool collection design.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to write tool descriptions that state purpose, timing, inputs, and outputs.
Shows when to consolidate overlapping tools into clearer workflows.
Covers response formats that balance detail with context efficiency.
Gives guidance for error messages that help agents recover.
Describes MCP naming conventions for fully qualified tool references.
Provides examples of strong and weak tool interface design.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not generate tool schemas automatically.
It does not test a live agent integration by itself.
It recommends broad tool patterns that require security controls in production.
It references supporting files that are not included in this package structure.

use_cases.title: Create New Agent Tools
use_cases.description: Design tool names, descriptions, parameters, return formats, and error messages before implementation.

use_cases.title: Review Tool Collections
use_cases.description: Find overlap, ambiguity, and missing recovery guidance across an existing tool set.

use_cases.title: Standardize MCP Tool Usage
use_cases.description: Create conventions for fully qualified MCP tool names and consistent parameter patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Assess One Tool
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single tool definition.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent tool definition for clarity. Check the name, description, inputs, outputs, and error messages. Suggest specific improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Error Recovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these tool errors from an agent workflow. Rewrite them so an agent can understand the problem and recover on the next attempt.

prompt_templates.title: Consolidate Tool Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this list of tools for overlap and selection ambiguity. Recommend which tools to merge, keep separate, or rename, with reasoning.

prompt_templates.title: Design Agent Tool Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture planning for a new agent system.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a tool architecture for this agent workflow. Include tool boundaries, naming conventions, response formats, safety controls, and test cases.

output_examples.input: Review a customer lookup tool with a vague description.
output: The tool purpose is unclear because it does not say when to use it.
The customer ID format should be documented with a concrete example.
The error response should explain whether the agent should retry or ask the user.

output_examples.input: Evaluate a tool collection with several search tools.
output: The collection has overlapping search behavior that may confuse tool selection.
Merge similar lookup actions when the agent cannot reliably choose between them.
Keep tools separate when they have different safety requirements or workflows.

output_examples.input: Plan MCP tool naming rules for a team.
output: Use fully qualified names that include the server prefix.
Document naming examples in the same place as tool descriptions.
Test prompts with multiple MCP servers enabled.

best_practices: Write tool descriptions that explain what the tool does, when to use it, and what it returns.
Use consistent names, parameter formats, and response shapes across related tools.
Add security boundaries before giving agents broad filesystem or command capabilities.
anti_patterns: Do not publish vague tools with names like search and undocumented parameters.
Do not split one workflow into many tools when selection boundaries are unclear.
Do not expose broad command execution without sandboxing, approval rules, and audit logging.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps design tool interfaces that agents can select, call, and recover from more reliably.

faq.question: Does it create working tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides design guidance, examples, and review criteria for tool builders.

faq.question: Is it specific to Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. The principles apply broadly, but the content includes guidance relevant to those tools.

faq.question: Why does it mention tool consolidation?
faq.answer: Consolidation reduces ambiguity when several tools could satisfy the same user request.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: Some guidance favors broad command and filesystem access. Production systems need limits and review controls.

faq.question: Can teams use this for MCP tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for fully qualified MCP tool names and consistent conventions.
